title: Χελώνη καὶ λαγωός
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 352 (= Perry 226). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« »). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Χελώνη καὶ λαγωὸς περὶ ὀξύτητος ἤριζον.
sentences.tr: Chelōnē kai lagōos peri oxytētos ērizon.
sentences.l: A tortoise and a hare about swiftness were-quarrelling.
sentences.i: A tortoise and a hare argued over which of them was the faster.
sentences.n: λαγωός is the older Attic form of 'hare' (later λαγώς; modern Greek λαγός). περί + genitive names what a dispute is about. The imperfect ἤριζον sets an open, ongoing quarrel — the aorists that follow will move the story in snapshots.

sentences.t: Καὶ δὴ προθεσμίαν στήσαντες καὶ τόπον ἀπηλλάγησαν.
sentences.tr: Kai dē prothesmian stēsantes kai topon apēllagēsan.
sentences.l: And so, a set-day having-fixed and a place, they parted.
sentences.i: So they fixed a day and a course, and parted.
sentences.n: προθεσμία is legal vocabulary — an appointed term, a deadline; the animals set up their race like a contract. στήσαντες: yet another aorist participle doing the narrative work before the main verb.

sentences.t: Ὁ μὲν οὖν λαγωὸς διὰ τὴν φυσικὴν ὠκύτητα ἀμελήσας τοῦ δρόμου, πεσὼν παρ' ὁδὸν ἐκοιμᾶτο.
sentences.tr: Ho men oun lagōos dia tēn physikēn ōkytēta amelēsas tou dromou, pesōn par' hodon ekoimato.
sentences.l: The hare then, because-of his natural swiftness having-neglected the running, having-lain-down by the road, was-sleeping.
sentences.i: The hare, trusting his natural speed, did not trouble himself about the race, but lay down by the roadside and slept.
sentences.n: Ὁ μέν here … Ἡ δέ opening the next sentence: the paired particles μέν … δέ are Greek's favorite way to set two parties against each other — you'll see the pair constantly. The imperfect ἐκοιμᾶτο paints the ongoing nap against the aorist snapshots around it.

sentences.t: Ἡ δὲ χελώνη συνειδυῖα ἑαυτῇ βραδύτητα, οὐ διέλιπε τρέχουσα, καὶ οὕτω κοιμώμενον τὸν λαγωὸν παραδραμοῦσα ἐπὶ τὸ βραβεῖον τῆς νίκης ἀφίκετο.
sentences.tr: Hē de chelōnē syneiduia heautēi bradytēta, ou dielipe trechousa, kai houtō koimōmenon ton lagōon paradramousa epi to brabeion tēs nikēs aphiketo.
sentences.l: But the tortoise, being-conscious in-herself of-slowness, did not leave-off running, and thus, the sleeping hare having-run-past, at the prize of the victory she arrived.
sentences.i: The tortoise, well aware of her own slowness, never stopped running — and so she slipped past the sleeping hare and came in for the victor's prize.
sentences.n: συνειδυῖα ἑαυτῇ, 'conscious in herself' — σύνοιδα with the dative reflexive is the Greek idiom for private self-knowledge. διαλείπω 'leave off' takes a participle: οὐ διέλιπε τρέχουσα, 'did not cease running.' βραβεῖον is the prize awarded by the βραβεύς, the umpire at the games.

sentences.t: Ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι πολλάκις φύσιν ἀμελοῦσαν πόνος ἐνίκησεν.
sentences.tr: Ho logos dēloi hoti pollakis physin amelousan ponos enikēsen.
sentences.l: The story shows that often a nature being-neglectful, toil has-conquered.
sentences.i: The story shows that hard work often defeats a natural gift that neglects itself.
sentences.n: The classic epimythium formula: ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι, 'the story shows that…'. ἐνίκησεν is a gnomic aorist — a past form stating a timeless truth, rendered by the English present. And note what the Greek moral is not: nothing about 'slow and steady.' The opposition is πόνος, toil, against a φύσις that ἀμελεῖ — talent that doesn't bother.
